We expect PDD to resume scaling its cost structure and improving profit margins. If Temu manages to break even, this would have a considerable positive effect on the consolidated earnings of the business.

Even if PDD's international venture fails to live up to its potential, at the current valuation level, we do not seem to be paying much for the growth potential of Temu. Therefore, we are not taking a significant risk for an uncertain outcome.

Risks

- While Temu is famed for its cheapness, the quality of products sold has at times been questionable. In order to continue building brand goodwill, Temu has to develop a value-for-money reputation, much like Costco did with its private labels. With a large number of SKUs and manufacturers, effective quality control might be challenging.

- Political and trade tensions between the U.S., EU, and China could escalate once again, making the cross-border direct-to-consumer retail challenging. Most recently, D. Trump was pushing allies to apply retaliatory tariffs to countries buying Russian oil. China has been spared so far, but this could change.

- The Chinese e-commerce market is very dynamic and competitive. As of late, TikTok parent Doyin has been gaining retail market share rapidly. Pinduoduo will need to continue to invest and innovate to maintain market share.

data.title: PDD Holdings: I Wouldn't Be A Buyer Here
data.description:
Pinduoduo and Temu are major players of affordable e-commerce in their respective markets. Both platforms are owned by PDD Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PDD) and so the company has become an international powerhouse. While there are consumer confidence concerns around the world, the stock has had a decent past six months, as shown below. This seems to indicate that their value-oriented product selection is helping them to manage headwinds rather well.

In the below analysis, it is shown that the company is actually feeling some of the effects of consumer weakness. Furthermore, factors like the revoking of the de minimis exemption will likely continue to weigh on profitability. With the sentiment in their domestic market also being shaky, I believe PDD Holdings' low valuation is justified and does not indicate a bargain. Therefore, I have decided to initiate the stock at a hold rating.

Top Line Growth Slows Again

PDD Holdings reported their FY2025 Q2 earnings in late August, and despite resilience, the direction of top line growth may be a concern in my view. The company as a whole generated revenues of RMB103.9848 billion, up 7% YoY. The USD figure differs slightly from platform to platform due to different exchange rates used but nonetheless growth in USD was also at 7%. As you can see in the chart above, the top line growth deceleration trend has continued, and Q2 represented the lowest growth of the past three years. Therefore, while there is a certain amount of resilience, the company has not been left unscathed by weak consumer sentiment. The company itself cited "intense competition" as the cause of the deceleration. Lastly, in USD terms they were able to beat expectations by $178.4 million, and so analysts were expecting a much softer quarter. Overall, given their selection of lower cost products, I would have expected even greater resilience from the company.

Marketing Is Main Growth Driver

When breaking down their revenues by segment, you can see that there is actually notable weakness in their sales of products. The company has two segments, online marketing services & others and transaction services. The former is the fees that sellers pay to be more prominent on the platform, while the latter is the commission the company makes for sales on the site.

For online marketing services & others, they saw a 13% increase in revenues to RMB55.7032 billion, while transaction services only saw growth of 0.7% to RMB48.2816 billion. This closer look clearly shows that consumer weakness is directly impacting the business. That said, sellers seem to still be finding the platform rather valuable as the increase in marketing revenues indicates that the popularity of the platform is robust. Therefore, in the long term, PDD Holdings' platforms will likely continue to grow.

Profitability Drops

Despite top line growth, PDD Holdings' operating income and EPS saw notable weakness. When looking at their costs and expenses, you can see that the main cause of this profitability weakness was due to the large increase in costs of revenue. For Q2, they reported RMB45.8589 billion, up 36% YoY. That far outpaces the revenue increase. They stated that this was due to "increased fulfilment fees, bandwidth and server costs, and payment processing fees." Moving forward, this may continue to be an issue. It was reported not too long ago that the Trump Administration is ending the de minimis exemption. Previously, PDD Holdings' Temu benefitted from this exemption as it made imports of packages that are worth less than $800 duty-free. Moving forward, the cost of doing business in the U.S. is going to be higher for PDD Holdings, and so there may not be an easy fix for the weakening in profitability.

In terms of the company's operations, there was actually an increase in efficiency. Operating expenses increased by just 5% to RMB32.333 billion. Furthermore, in the breakdown of these expenses, you can see that general and administrative expenses actually dropped. R&D expenses on the other hand increased by 23% to RMB3.5913 billion and so the company is investing a healthy amount for future growth. Still, due to the unfavorable developments for cost of revenue, non-GAAP operating income dropped by 21% while adjusted EPS dropped from RMB23.24 to RMB22.07. While this may seem a bit disappointing, in USD terms they still beat EPS expectations by $1.03 and so results were much better than feared. PDD Holdings stated that "as [they] remain focused on long-term value creation, the sustained investments may continue to weigh on short-term profitability." The revoking of de minimis is also unfavorable, and so investors should expect a rather long wait for profitability to bounce back.

China Consumer Confidence

By now, we all know that consumer confidence in the U.S. isn't great due to persistent inflation worries and, even more importantly, tariffs. China has historically been a major market for products, and until a few years ago, the strength of the consumer was solid. After the plunge in the China Consumer Confidence Index in early 2022, there has been minimal recovery, and according to the latest data, sentiment has remained subdued in the country.

Firstly, as I said at the beginning, the fact that Pinduoduo is focused on cheaper products makes the company better positioned to weather this weakness. However, from what we have seen, they haven't been able to escape these effects, and with consumer confidence in their domestic market not showing many signs of improving at this point, I would say that the outlook only looks murky at best as of now.

Valuation Is Low For A Reason

The forward P/E ratio is currently at 13.73 and while this valuation is low, I would be hesitant to call the stock a bargain at this point. The multiple is far below the peak of late 2023 and note that the stock trades at a significant discount to the Consumer Discretionary sector's median non-GAAP forward P/E of 17.80. However, after the above analysis, you should have a good idea of the near term challenges that are present.

Firstly, top line growth has slowed to multiyear lows. Secondly, profitability is getting eroded, and the end of the de minimis exemption poses a very really concern. Lastly, consumers in China remain very hesitant to spend. With all of these factors impacting the company, I would say that the outlook is less than desirable in the short to medium term. The increase in marketing revenues shows that the platform remains popular, but I believe current challenges make the low valuation appropriate. I would conclude that PDD Holdings is around fairly valued at current levels.

Technical Analysis

Overall, the technical setup for PDD Holdings is still quite strong. For the long term, you have the 50-day SMA above the 200 day SMA and the gap between those lines is still expanding. Therefore, bullish momentum seems to be going strong. As of now, negative divergence fears are only moderate, as the September high in the stock coincided with notable highs in both the MACD and RSI indicators. For the near term, most signals are also bullish. The 9-day EMA is above the 21-day EMA by a healthy margin, and the stock is also a fair amount above the Bollinger Bands midline. While the MACD seems a bit indecisive currently, the RSI still has a reading of 66.34. This reading far above 50 shows that the bulls are in comfortable control of the stock. Therefore, despite the only neutral fundamental outlook for the company, traders may want to take advantage of technical strength in the near term.

Concluding Remarks

PDD Holdings' Pinduoduo is an e-commerce leader in China, and Temu is becoming a major player in international markets. However, at this point, the unfavorable environment is a major deterrent. Consumer weakness around the world is a concern, and the end of the de minimis exemption is another headwind. Therefore, I expect an extended period of uncertainty and subdued growth for the company, and so the valuation may be justifiably low currently. While the technical setup may be attractive to traders, for most investors, I believe PDD Holdings is only a hold at this point.

data.title: Motorcar Parts Of America: Shares Are Cheap Enough And Growth Attractive Enough To Be Bullish
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $336.9 million as of this writing, Motorcar Parts of America (NASDAQ:MPAA) is an interesting business that flies under the radar of the investment community more broadly. In recent years, management has achieved attractive growth on the top line. But bottom line results have been mixed. For this year, management expects growth to continue. And on an absolute basis, shares are priced at levels that I would consider to be fairly attractive. Although it's not the best of the best in terms of opportunities, I do think it justifies a soft 'buy' market at this time.

Digging into Motorcar Parts of America

According to the management team at Motorcar Parts of America, the company operates as a supplier of automotive aftermarket non discretionary replacement parts and test solutions, as well as of related diagnostic equipment. This is part of a $130 billion automotive aftermarket space if we focus on just North America alone. Management acknowledges that this market is really broken up into two separate categories. The first would be the do-it-yourself market that is catered to by retail chain outlets and online resellers. And the second is the professional installer market that consists of major distributors, dealer networks, and more. Management focuses on selling to both of these spaces.

On top of this, it also services the emerging global market for automotive test solutions and diagnostic equipment that is estimated to be worth around $11 billion, and it also plays in the $40 billion market for medium- and heavy duty automotive aftermarket replacement parts. This includes not only trucks, but also industrial equipment, equipment in the marine space, and in the agricultural market.

Digging deeper, the real sweet spot for the company when it comes to the US market would be rotating electric products. They claim they have about a 49% stake in what they estimate is a $3.18 billion market opportunity. But they also have significant market share and other niches. Examples would be a 16% share of the $940 million wheel hubs market, a 27% share of the $1.27 billion brake calipers market, a 12% share of the brake master cylinders market that's worth $638 million, and a 33% share of the $358 million brake power boosters market. They also play in the $9.81 billion brake pads and rotors industry.

The company accomplishes all of this through its significant physical network. What's more, it is continuing to make investments in expanding, with recent examples being a 410,000 square foot distribution center, 370,000 square feet of manufacturing and core sorting for the brake calipers market that is split between two separate buildings, and changes it is making to 312,000 square foot facility in Mexico. This year, the company even added a new warehousing and distribution facility in Malaysia to cater to its direct shipment programs for the future.

Over the past few years now, management has achieved consistent top line growth. Revenue grew from $683.1 million in 2023 to $757.4 million in 2025. According to management, this rise in revenue was mostly the result of increasing demand for its products, with the increase from 2024 to 2025 stemming largely from greater demand for its rotating electric and brake related product lines.

Although the increase in revenue is great to see, bottom line results for the business have been much more volatile. The firm's net loss ballooned from $4.2 million in 2023 to $49.2 million in 2024. But then, in 2025, the net loss narrowed to $19.5 million. A lot of this volatility can be attributed to foreign currency fluctuations. As an example, in 2023, the company saw a $9.3 million benefit from foreign currency fluctuations. But in 2025, it reported a $15.9 million hit associated with them. But even if we look at other profitability metrics, the picture has been mixed.

It is true that operating cash flow went from negative $21.8 million in 2023 to positive $45.5 million in 2025. But when we strip out changes in working capital, we get a decline from $42.9 million in 2023 to $36.8 million in 2024 before experiencing a jump up to $54.4 million in 2025. Similar volatility can be seen when looking at EBITDA, with the figure growing from $48.9 million to $58.6 million before ultimately contracting to $50.3 million in the latest fiscal year.

For the current fiscal year, we do have data covering one operating quarter. During that quarter, which would be the first quarter of 2026, revenue came in at $188.4 million. That's an increase of 10.9% compared to the $169.9 million the company reported the previous year. Based on the data provided, this is because of strong demand for its rotating electrical and brake related products.

This rise in revenue brought with it improvements on the bottom line across the board. For starters, the firm went from a net loss of $18.1 million to a net gain of $3 million. Some of this can be chalked up to an improvement in the firm's gross profit margin from 17.2% to 18% thanks to greater facility utilization, cost savings initiatives, and favorable changes in product mix. The picture here would have been better had it not been for a $1.4 million hit that the company experienced because of tariffs. A lot of the bottom line impact, however, came from foreign currency fluctuations. These went from a negative impact last year of $11.1 million to a positive impact this year of $8.3 million. However, general and administrative costs fell nicely from 9.8% of sales to 6.7% thanks to management reducing headcount in a move that saved at $2.9 million and other miscellaneous factors. Operating cash flow went from negative $20.8 million to positive $10 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from $2.4 million to $8.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from negative $1.1 million to positive $20.7 million.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety, management expects revenue to come in at between $800 million and $820 million. At the midpoint, this would represent an increase of 6.9% over what the company saw last year. There will be some uncertainty when it comes to the bottom line, largely because of tariffs. Although the company saw $1.4 million net impact associated with tariffs in the most recent quarter, in the final quarter of last year the impact was larger at $4.6 million. They said that in the second quarter of 2026, they do expect the impact to be larger than what they saw in the first quarter of the year. But management continues to work on tariff mitigation strategies like price increases.

Management has estimated that operating income this year will grow by between 4.3% and 10.4%, with some of that attributable to the firm's decision to pass through costs onto its customers stemming from tariffs, and some of it related to cost mitigation strategies. If we take the midpoint here and apply that to cash flow metrics, we would anticipate adjusted operating cash flow of $58.4 million and EBITDA of $54 million. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results from 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently valued. On an absolute basis, these are very attractive multiples to me. And in the table below, I stacked the enterprise up against five other similar firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that two of the five were cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, four of the five ended up being cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Motorcar Parts of America 6.2 8.9
Holley (HLLY) 11.1 8.1
Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) 3.8 6.1
Stoneridge (SRI) 4.2 14.7
Cooper-Standard Holdings (CPS) 9.2 7.6
Autoliv (ALV) 10.1 7.8

In addition to the stock being attract lovely priced on an absolute basis, there is another catalyst at play that could create value for shareholders in the long run. This is the fact that vehicles have, over time, stayed on the road longer than at any point in the past. Some of this is because of improvements in technology. But some of it can be attributed to the rising cost of new vehicles. The great thing about this is that, in theory, a rise in the average age of vehicles should result in greater demand for aftermarket parts. That should certainly bode well for a business such as this.

It is worth noting that this company, like pretty much any other company out there, is not without risks. In the past, for instance, the company has struggled to break even from a profitability perspective even though cash flows have been largely positive. In each of the last three completed fiscal years, the firm generated a net loss, with a cumulative net loss during that time of $72.9 million. Because GAAP net income operates under an accrual method, a case could be made that over the long run, if nothing changes, this would be indicative of the ultimate cash flow picture of the business. At some point, this is something that management will need to resolve. But with a small net profit in the most recent quarter of $3 million compared to the $18.1 million loss reported the previous year, and with cash flow metrics improving as I detailed already, this company specific risk is certainly easing.

Investors should also be cognizant of the fact that this is a highly competitive industry with low margins. The net losses the company has exhibited are reflective of this. Add on top of the competitive environment the fact that the company is highly exposed to commodity prices like steel, and it's not unthinkable that wild changes in pricing associated with relevant commodities, whether due to tariffs or other issues, could prove problematic for the company. I would argue that perhaps one upside of the direction that the economy is heading because of tariffs is that a recessionary environment should in theory help to lower prices of some commodities. But there are many working parts here that could work against the company as well, such as the impact that tariffs have on pricing even as a recession occurs.

And obviously, another thing that investors should be aware of is the fact that a large portion of the firm's revenue comes from three key customers. During the 2025 fiscal year, for instance, its three largest customers accounted for 86% of its revenue, with the largest responsible for 39%. This exposes the company to significant pressure from its customers, and could end up significantly impairing financial performance should one or more of those major customers shift to another supplier.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I would argue that Motorcar Parts of America remains an attractive prospect. Shares of the business seem to be priced around the midpoint of other similar enterprises. But on an absolute basis, the company is appealing. Management continues to grow revenue, though I do wish that cash flow figures were more consistent. Given this combination of factors, I think that it does justify a soft 'buy' rating at this moment.

data.title: HPI: High Leverage Limits Appeal
data.description:
Overview

Income funds can be a great way to hedge your portfolio against the uncertainties and volatility that traditional equities may expose investors to. John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:HPI) aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of preferred securities and corporate bonds. When I previously covered HPI, I issued a hold rating due to the premium valuation at the time. Since my last coverage, HPI has released an updated annual report that prompted me to revisit the fund and take another look at its performance and outlook over the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HPI's share price has declined by about 8.44%. The fund failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months but did see its share price spike in anticipation of the Fed's interest rate cut. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return offsets these declines by essentially breaking even over the same time frame. HPI now offers investors a high dividend yield of 8.5% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. This makes the fund an attractive choice for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income.

Although I like the fund's portfolio and structure, I believe that the heavy use of leverage is suppressing the share price and internal earnings at this time. Since we remain in a higher interest rate environment, the fund's net investment income levels have fallen from the levels in its prior years. Although the Fed did implement a small interest rate cut in September, I believe there need to be several additional cuts to make HPI more attractive. Despite the challenges, HPI now trades at the higher end of its premium-to-NAV range. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that HPI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fact sheet, HPI has total managed assets of $677.2M that are spread across 152 different holdings. HPI's strategy is to invest at least 50% of its assets into investment-grade securities, which helps put an emphasis on reliable income generation. The fund mostly focuses on preferred stocks, which offer better price stability and income generation compared to traditional equities. The full breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Preferred Securities: 55.65%
  • Corporate Bonds: 41.35%
  • US Government Agency: 2.32%
  • Cash: 0.68%

Looking at the breakdown by sector composition, we can see that HPI is heavily focused on investments in the Financials sector at 61.5% of assets. This means that HPI may present some concentration risk, which makes the fund more vulnerable to negative shifts within the Financial sector. This is followed by exposure to the Utilities and Energy sectors, which account for 21.1% and 5.4% of assets, respectively. However, the combination of the top two sectors makes up approximately 82.6% of total assets, so investors should anticipate these sectors to have the greatest impact on HPI's daily price movement and total return potential.

Since the fund is most heavily focused on the Financials sector, we can see many large-cap banks in HPI's top holdings. For instance, Bank of America (BAC) is the largest holding at 5.66% of assets. This is followed by other notable names such as Wells Fargo (WFC), Morgan Stanley (MS), The Goldman Sachs Group (GS), and Citigroup (C), just to name a few. However, it's important to remember that HPI holds these positions through preferred securities rather than common stock. Therefore, investors should limit their expectations around HPI's ability to provide capital appreciation over time.

Performance Risk

Just to demonstrate the fund's price weakness, I pulled in a performance chart over the last decade. As we can see below, HPI's share price has declined by 10.24% over the last ten years. This means that the fund has essentially missed out on the bull markets over the last decade and caused investors to see the value of their position decline. When including all distributions paid out, the total return jumps to nearly 102% over the same period. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their return will come from HPI's ability to continue paying out distributions.

The problem is that when a fund relies on distributions, it typically offers very little downside protection. There have been several instances in the past where HPI is paying out more than it actually generates in income. A good example of this can be seen when measuring the performance over the course of 2022's decline. Market indexes declined in response to record-breaking inflationary data and aggressive interest rate hikes implemented.

As we can see below, HPI experienced a total loss of 15.77%, despite offering investors a high dividend yield. Therefore, it's important to understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically equal a high return. Although HPI still saw a smaller loss than a more traditional index-tracking ETF, such as SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), the fund still didn't protect investors against the market's downward trend.

I believe a lot of the fund's weakness comes from the fact that interest rates are still high. The Fed recently implemented a 25 basis point cut to rates, but they still remain near the higher end of their range over the last decade. This is important because HPI utilizes leverage as part of its operating strategy, which is used as a way to amplify earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 37.96% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $257M.

I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this period of elevated rates, and it can drastically increase the risk profile of the fund. Investors need to consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to a security that doesn't perform as anticipated. Since interest rates remain elevated, the spread between HPI's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities can be smaller. This makes it a lot harder to utilize leverage efficiently.

Another risk comes from the fact that HPI has a sizeable exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, a borrower with a credit rating below a BBB is classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on debt financing to fuel operations. The lower the credit rating, the higher the probability of being exposed to defaults. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 53.88%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 41.09%
  • Not Rated: 2.03%

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, HPI traded at a small premium to NAV of 3%. Following the recent price rally, HPI now trades at a larger premium to NAV of approximately 5.92%. For reference, HPI has traded at an average premium to NAV of only 1.96% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current premium in relation to its historical range, I added the red line to the chart below. As we can see, HPI now trades at the higher end of its premium to NAV range over the last ten-year period, which may indicate that it may not be a good time to accumulate shares.

Since the time of my last coverage, HPI has released an updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $1.05 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.32 per share. What I like most about HPI is that the net investment income levels have remained consistent over the last few years. However, we should note that the current net investment income levels sit lower than the levels seen when interest rates were much lower in 2021 and 2022. This may be due to the thinner operating spread related to the fund's leverage.

One of the issues is that the net investment income levels aren't sufficient enough to cover distributions on their own. This leaves the fund reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support payouts. However, net realized gains can only be captured during periods where markets are trending upwards so that HPI can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased. This dependence has caused HPI's NAV to remain suppressed over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.81 per share
  • 2022: $17.99 per share
  • 2023: $15.31 per share
  • 2024: $16.13 per share
  • 2025: $16.02 per share

Therefore, I believe that HPI will become more attractive once interest rates are cut and the net investment income levels can be improved. The most ideal scenario would be for net investment income levels to completely support distributions on their own. This can be achieved with a slight reduction in payouts or by increasing its income generation, which is harder to achieve.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1235 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.5%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.482 per share. HPI has demonstrated that it can easily support the distributions when market conditions are positive. The issue arises when markets are trending downwards and the fund experiences net realized losses. Referring back to the same annual report, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $3.86 per share
  • 2022: ($1.34) per share
  • 2023: ($1.20) per share
  • 2024: $2.30 per share
  • 2025: $1.37 per share

As we can see, HPI only generated enough income to support distributions in two of the last five years. While there are certainly periods where HPI can generate earnings strong enough to cover several years' worth of distributions, such as 2021, these moments seem reliant on lower interest rates or strong market rallies. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that HPI has kept distributions the same since 2020. Although there was a dividend cut at the end of 2019, the fund has still demonstrated that it prioritizes consistent payouts through different market environments.

It's worth noting that the distributions received from HPI are mostly funded by net investment income. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 68% of the distributions paid YTD were funded by net investment income. The remaining 32% was fueled by return of capital distributions. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in HPI may be best implemented within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on HPI at this time. The fund trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range, and the earnings are suppressed due to the higher interest rate environment. The fund has failed to generate enough earnings to support payouts in 2025. Although payouts have remained consistent over time, this has negatively impacted HPI's ability to grow its NAV per share. The fund utilizes a large amount of leverage, and I believe it is too costly to do so at this time. Therefore, I believe that the fund's appeal will only increase after interest rates are substantially lower.
